text: Decreased adenosine triphosphate (ATP) pool describes the loss of balance between ATP synthesis and ATP consumption, leading to reduced total ATP. As a primary form of biological energy, ATP is used by many biological processes  ADDIN EN.CITE <EndNote><Cite><Author>Bonora&lt 2012)</DisplayText><record><rec-num app="EN" db-id="5e2w9wptc29tdlevdxip9vx55d22fvzrfere&q timestamp="1606514843">4190</key&g name="Journal Article">17</ref-type><contributo Massimo</author><author>Patergnani, Simone</author><author>Rimessi, Alessandro</author><author>De Marchi, Elena</author><author>Suski, Jan M.</author><author>Bononi, Angela</author><author>Giorgi, Carlotta</author><author>Marchi, Saverio</author><author>Missiroli, Sonia</author><author>Poletti, Federica</author><author>Wieckowski, Mariusz R.</author><author>Pinton, Paolo</author></authors></contribut synthesis and storage</title><secondary-title>Purine Signalling</secondary-title></titles>& Signalling</full-title></periodical>&l 2012). Decrease in ATP level normally attributes to metabolic disorders in major ATP synthetic pathways, such as mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, fatty acid β-oxidation, glycolysis and plant photophosphorylation.
